Ladies and gentlemen, video number three inside of phase number three, the products and services category, we're going to color transactional services versus recurring services and how you're going to be able to put what under where and how identify this kind of stuff to have the biggest and best success inside of your business. Now, I'm going to tell you how to kind of balance this first, okay? Recurring services, let's get clear on definition. Recurring services means somebody is spending money with you every month, meaning if they sign up on the first of the month, they're spending X amount of dollars again in 30 days. You can also do these things by building up a long process that you charge them when milestones are completed. So if you have a big project, like a web development project, you have the ability to break it down into four different milestones where somebody pays X amount then, then, then, and then accumulating to X number. So that can be a recurring service too. Now what you want to understand about recurring services is that this is what's going to be the backbone of you being able to stay in business. I've found that my ability to just stay afloat is my ability to have a foundation of people who are paying X amount of dollars a month. For me to just pay my overhead in business for what it takes for me to stay in business a month at this point is about $27,000 to $36,000 per month in payroll because I have a 19 person staff in software costs, in advertising costs, in executive costs, commissions, whatever it is that I'm paying in business, that's what my monthly expenses are currently. So I need to make that amount each month at a minimum. Now I have clients that spend $25,000, that spend between $100,000 on a month to month basis, that spend on the low end of $4,000 a month. I have one guy that's grandfathered on one of those original plans I mentioned at $18,000 a month. I keep him around because he's a great case study for us. Now, these are all people who pay on a monthly basis. So the great thing I can tell you about recurring services as well is that space these things out throughout the month. Don't just have them all come in at the first. Just come in whatever throughout the month, man. It's great to have something on the first, then another on the fourth, and then maybe again on the ninth, and then another recurring payment on the 15th, and then two more on the 18th and 20th, and then three more that come in on the 23rd, and then one more or two more that come in between that time and the 31st in a month. That's fantastic. That, ladies and gentlemen, is where you wake up and you have a big lump sum in your account and you have the ability to always stay liquid. And you have the ability when emergencies pop up or when you need to add in your team member or when your computer breaks and you got to go buy something or when your team member has to fly to so-and-so to go close so-and-so or whatever comes out and pops up or just happens, you have the money to pay for it and it's comfortable for you. You don't have to be stressed to have that financial strain that most people have. And you want to do that once you get through recurring revenue. You can do those things like in the last video I just showed you guys on different products that you can often assign to your business. You can show people things like a social services package for your SMMA type business. Or if you're a digital agency and DA, you have the ability to go out and close people between 8,000 and 12,000. Sometimes you can get up to my level and 15,000 and 25,000. If you guys work with somebody who spends a lot of money on ads, maybe you want to do a percentage of ad spend, which I don't recommend. But you can if somebody is spending a lot of money on ads because the revenue is incentivized to get them to spend more to make money. There's a lot of things that you can do here. I also recommend after a different period of time in my business, after I became really confident to have what's called a success fee, you can do this whenever you're comfortable doing it. It's really about the confidence you have when you're pitching a deal is what I found gets the deal to happen right now. Most people are willing to give you a percentage of, hey, if you do good, we'll pay you on that. Now, here's the thing. What's the percentage that you should feel comfortable charging? Low end 10% high end 30%. You can joke around and you can say 30% like Apple and if they say no, we don't want to do any less, you can say, well, we don't negotiate with terrorists. And if they kind of say, okay, well, that's fine. That's great. You just got 30%. If you do anything less, like I said, I wouldn't do anything below 10%. Don't let this become a negotiation tactic where somebody leverages that against you in order to lower your service fee. You want to always tell the person that you are non-negotiable on the service fee that you present them with because you have to cover payroll, software costs, and you have a built-in profit per deal where it makes sense to do it to take on their client account. Because you have several other people you're considering for the same position that you're going to fill them into within your business in that client slot and you're not going to negotiate the client fee. Otherwise, you're going to have to take on a different person in business. That's how you want to always retain the service fee. What you want to do once again is you want to have a service fee just so I can write this down with you guys at Visual World Plus Percentage. So otherwise known as a success fee. Now, success fees are fantastic. These are things once again that come in and get you that extra dollar on a monthly basis on top of the service fee. So sometimes if I want to have leverage of bringing on a client, these are the different negotiating tactics that I use. Say something like, hey, I'd love for you to sign up for a $12,000 package and I'd love to take a 30% stake of the revenue that we can generate for you in business. But I'll tell you what, if you sign up with us today, I'll lower that 30% down to 25%. I don't keep the 12k with where it's at. Let's do it. You know? Or you can say, I'll lower the service fee $2,000 if you sign up today and I'll keep that rev share at 30%. Or if you don't want to do service fees, you can just take a large percentage of business, but I really wouldn't do that once again unless the person is just not budging at all and you're bulletproof confident that you can generate business for them on the success fee. Like literally, you've calculated the time into it, you're 100% going to get it. And any type of deal that you ever do, put it in an agreement. Don't be a dummy. We're going to have a whole video on that in a minute talking about agreements. Make sure you watch that. You don't need to understand it. Transactional services, completely different and recurring services. Now you do have the ability to offer every single thing that you offer as a service as a transaction. Meaning you could do it one time for somebody who only wants something for a month to help with their position. You could offer Facebook ads for a month or consulting for a month or trading for a month or just one thing for a month or you could just do one email blast or whatever you want to do. That's all fun and games, but it doesn't get you as paid as what recurring does and it doesn't get you the ability to generate as much of a relationship level of rapport that you'll be able to take it to if you do the recurring deal. That matters because you're going to get more and more sales, you're going to get more or you're going to have more ability to get greater testimonials and you're not going to open yourself up to as many liabilities to hurt your reputation by exposing yourself to taking on more clients. Transactional services can be things like PR, they can be things like influencer marketing deals, they can be things like one-off services like you talk to one website or one landing page or one funnel or one email campaign or one ad campaign or whatever. Anything, one set of graphics that the person needs done, one project that they need complete before their big event, whatever. Transactional services, these things are a heavy hitter value place, meaning if I'm doing a transaction, my intention is to get you on some type of recurring agreement. I don't want to keep you at a transaction. I want to build so much value with you here that it leverages you into a relationship with me here. That's my goal. You see, if I have something, let's say like a DAP ticket I'm giving you today, right? $300 for three-day live stream access, immense value just to sit here and watch because you also get to have conversations with me in between the actual filming, right? On top of that, you get access as the first individual to be the one to log into this program and see it before everybody else does. I'm literally never going to sell the DAP by itself. Incredible value, right? You also get to be the first one to ask me questions in real time that retains your business and technically get mentorship just during the three-day ticket time. Take that in. That's immense value. In my opinion, that makes somebody when I say, hey, I have a $4,000 monthly recurring package where I'm going to take a percent of your business, but more importantly, I'm going to help you and be an actual mentor for you where you can have custom questions asked all day long via text email. We have a one-hour weekly call. You also get the group of peer-to-peers. It's the ultimate way to run your business, right? The person who's considering that would absolutely do it because they're negotiating a percentage of their business that they don't even have yet. If I take 30% of somebody's business, well, they're negotiating 70% of something that doesn't exist yet. To them, the amount of money they'll make off 70% is far greater than what they never make by themselves because they already have somebody who can help scale the result. That's immense value, but you have to warm them up before they just jump into that kind of decision unless they already have rapport with you in the sales process for the market. There will be some people who immediately jump into this if they're qualified, but there will be five students at a time who need to come into that. Apply this to your business. What can you do on a transactional basis that delivers immense value to somebody to make them want to go into a recurring relationship with? What is it that you can sell to somebody that's quick, efficient, easy, and does the job for somebody in that over-exchange and that abundance category? Excuse me, in that abundance category. What are you going to do in business to weigh out your transactions versus your recovery? Are you going to have one or the other? Are you going to just be transactions? Understand that you have to have more yeses if you're doing transactions compared to the less yeses we do in our current maintenance. How much do you have to understand, ladies and gentlemen? If you so choose to take this route of my greatest advice to you throughout this whole program, it would be to have a lower level of higher paying, returning, and monthly customers and have some transactions that are also high dollar values that are absolutely the most efficient, easiest thing that is already a part of your day as a one-time transaction developer as well. Have a small stable client that paid you a ton of money. Don't go through the same mistakes I did of having a ton of people flow through your business just to get transactions or to get low recurring payments and tax-as-cycle duty workers you can't deliver because you're undercharging. Charge a lot. Have a small stable people. Have transactions to balance the afternoon recurring payments are there or when you're filling up your business and make sure you have a synergy between your transactional services and your recurring services. Also, don't be afraid to take a percentage of people to business when you're charging a service fee plus a success fee and do not forget that your service fee is non-negotiable. You need that for payroll cost and you're going to need that for every other cost and you factor that into the deal because it's necessary in order for you to be able to do everything upfront for the individual without you being stressed out handling their client account. Make sure you voice that to them. This was video number two. We've been in number three inside of the products and services category. This was transactional services versus recurring services. See you in the next video, ladies and gentlemen.
